Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} | image = Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg | caption = Suwa Jinja in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|134|13|53|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | name = Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg|300px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 2048.jpg|Stone steps File:Suwazinzya2.jpg|Autumn foliage at the base of the stone steps File:Suwazinzya1.jpg|Autumn foliage == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Suwa-jinja (Chizu)|Suwa Shrine}} * [[List of Cultural Properties of Tottori Prefecture]] == External Links == * [http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ Suwa Shrine] Chizu Town Tourism Association, accessed April 29, 2015 * [http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Chizu Town Tourism Association, accessed April 29, 2015 * [http://site5.tori-info.co.jp/p/chizukyu/guide/chizu/suwajinjya/ Suwa Shrine] Chizu Express, accessed April 29, 2015 * [http://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/448 Suwa Shrine] Tottori Prefecture Tourism Federation, accessed April 29, 2015 {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine}} [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:History of Chizu, Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 2048.jpg|Stone steps File:Suwazinzya2.jpg|Autumn foliage at the base of the stone steps File:Suwazinzya1.jpg|Autumn foliage == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Suwa-jinja The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} | image = Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg | caption = Suwa Jinja in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|134|13|53|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | name = Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg|300px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 2048.jpg|Stone steps File:Suwazinzya2.jpg|Autumn foliage at the base of the stone steps File:Suwazinzya1.jpg|Autumn foliage == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Suwa-jinja (Chizu)|Suwa Shrine}} * [[List of Cultural Properties of Tottori Prefecture]] == External Links == * [http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ Suwa Shrine] Chizu Town Tourism Association, {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} | image = Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg | caption = Suwa Jinja in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|134|13|53|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | name = Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg|300px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 2048.jpg|Stone steps File:Suwazinzya2.jpg|Autumn foliage at the base of the stone steps File:Suwazinzya1.jpg|Autumn foliage == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Suwa-jinja (Chizu)|Suwa Shrine}} * [[List of Cultural Properties of Tottori Prefecture]] == External Links == * [http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ Suwa Shrine] Chizu Town Tourism Association, accessed April 29, 2015 * [http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Chizu Town Tourism Association, accessed April 29, 2015 * [http://site5.tori-info.co.jp/p/chizukyu/guide/chizu/suwajinjya/ Suwa Shrine] Chizu Express, accessed April 29, 2015 * [http://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/448 Suwa Shrine] Tottori Prefecture Tourism Federation, accessed April 29, 2015 {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chizu, Tottori]] [[Category:Buildings and Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 2048.jpg|Stone steps File:Suwazinzya2.jpg|Autumn foliage at the base of the stone steps File:Suwazinzya1.jpg|Autumn foliage == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Suwa-jinja The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} | image = Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg | caption = Suwa Jinja in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|134|13|53|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | name = Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg|300px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 2048.jpg|Stone steps File:Suwazinzya2.jpg|Autumn foliage at the base of the stone steps File:Suwazinzya1.jpg|Autumn foliage == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Suwa-jinja {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} | image = Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg | caption = Suwa Jinja in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|134|13|53|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | name = Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg|300px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 2048.jpg|Stone steps File:Suwazinzya2.jpg|Autumn foliage at the base of the stone steps File:Suwazinzya1.jpg|Autumn foliage == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Suwa-jinja {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} | image = Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg | caption = Suwa Jinja in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|16|16|N|134|13|53|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | name = Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Chidu suwajinja01 1920.jpg|300px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall | location = Miyamae, Chizu, [[Chizu Town]], [[Yazu District]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami | Shrine rank = | established = [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) | main hall = | alternate names = | festival = [[April 27]], [[October 27]] | ceremony = Hashira festival (every 6 years) }} {{nihongo|'''Suwa Jinja'''|諏訪神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Known from the time of the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions]], the shrine is celebrated for its [[Zelkova serrata|Zelkova]] and [[momiji]]. The [[Honden]] dates to 1832.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/suwa/ |title=Suwa Jinja |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The Onbashira [[Matsuri|festival]], held in the [[Tiger (zodiac)|Year of the Tiger]] and [[Rooster (zodiac)|Year of the Rooster]], sees pillars of [[Japanese cedar]] carried through the town.{{cite web |url=http://cms.sanin.jp/p/chizu/kankou/rekishi_miru/hashira/ |title=Suwa Jinja Onbashira |publisher=[[Chizu, Tottori|Chizu Town]] |accessdate=23 April 2011}} The shrine was founded in [[1278]] (1st year of [[Kōan (Kamakura period)|Kōan]]) and was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]]. During the [[Edo period]], it flourished as a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. Situated at the base of Mount Ushigase, the approach to the shrine connects with the Chizu Highway. The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02 2048.jpg|Stone steps File:Suwazinzya2.jpg|Autumn foliage at the base of the stone steps File:Suwazinzya1.jpg|Autumn foliage == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Suwa-jinja The precincts are home to hundreds of maple trees, the shrine's emblem, which attract many visitors during the autumn foliage season. == History == * [[1278]] (1st year of Kōan) - The shrine was established to enshrine a branch spirit from [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]] under the name "Suwa Daimyōjin." * [[1583]] (11th year of Tenshō), December - The shrine was burned down during Toyotomi Hideyoshi's campaign against Mount Yodo. * Edo period - It became a prayer site for the [[Ikeda clan]], the lords of the [[Tottori Domain]]. * [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei) - The Hashira festival began. * [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō), November - The main hall was rebuilt. * [[1868]] (4th year of Keiō), August - The shrine was renamed "Suwa Shrine" following the addition of new deities. * [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji), January - The Heiden, Haiden, and Tsuri-den were reconstructed. == Grounds == * **main hall** - Rebuilt in [[1832]] (3rd year of Tenpō) * **Heiden and Haiden** - Rebuilt in [[1904]] (37th year of Meiji) == Hashira festival == Since [[1782]] (2nd year of Tenmei), the "Hashira festival," modeled after the [[Onbashira festival]] of [[Suwa Taisha]] in [[Shinano Province]], has been held every six years in the years of the Monkey and Tiger in the lunar calendar. During the festival, four cedar logs are cut from the designated mountains, paraded through the town, and erected at the four corners of the main hall. On March 23, [[2001]] (13th year of Heisei), the Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property by Tottori Prefecture[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/FD8385FB128EC7254925796F0007FF81?OpenDocument Suwa Shrine Hashira festival] Tottori Prefecture Education Committee, accessed April 29, 2015[https://www1.town.chizu.tottori.jp/chizu/kyouiku/27/bunkazai/ Cultural Properties of Chizu Town] Chizu Town, accessed April 29, 2015. == Cultural Properties == === Tottori Prefecture Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * **Hashira festival of Suwa Shrine** - Designated on March 23, 2001 (13th year of Heisei) === Chizu Town Protected Cultural Property === * **map of Chizu Inn** (Ancient Document) - Designated on November 25, 1987 (62nd year of Shōwa) == Notable Individuals == * **Kokubei Taishi** - A sculptor. His mother was the daughter of a Suwa Shrine priest. == Access == * 15-minute walk from [[Chizu Station]] on the [[Chizu Express]] or JR [[Inbi Line]] == Gallery == File:Chidu suwajinja02
